serving.natural_language_portion: One standard slice of banana cake weighing approximately 100 g, as commonly served in Singapore cafes and bakeries.

nutrient_source.title: Banana Cake — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, iron) sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo at confidence 0.95. Secondary micronutrients (cholesterol, saturated fat, trans fat, vitamin C) were supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for banana cake (NDB No. 18009 / closest analogue) at confidence 0.70, as these fields were not reported in the HPB record. The USDA values are consistent with the HPB macro profile.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — banana cake with sugar
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI value of 51 (low) for banana cake with sugar is directly listed in the University of Sydney GI Database and the Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables. Glycemic load per 100 g serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g) / 100 = (51 × 36.2) / 100 = 18.5, consistent with the input record.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (21.2 g/100 g) with low dietary fibre (1.3 g/100 g) promotes insulin-mediated sodium retention; sodium is 143 mg/100 g.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit banana cake to a small occasional portion (30–40 g) because the high refined sugar and low fibre combination drives insulin-mediated sodium retention, compounding the direct sodium load of 143 mg/100 g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar 21.2 g/100 g exceeds the 20 g/100 g glycaemic ceiling; carbohydrate 36.2 g/100 g with only 1.3 g/100 g fibre yields a glycaemic load of 18.5 per 100 g serving.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid or strictly limit banana cake to 20–30 g on rare occasions, as the high sugar content (21.2 g/100 g) and glycaemic load of 18.5 will cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation; if baking at home, substituting half the sugar with a low-GI sweetener and using wholemeal flour can reduce glycaemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat 17.4 g/100 g with saturated fat 3.0 g/100 g and trace trans fat 0.1 g/100 g; high sugar (21.2 g/100 g) contributes to dyslipidaemia and triglyceride elevation.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, banana cake should be consumed only occasionally and in small portions (30–40 g) because the combination of saturated fat (3.0 g/100 g), trace trans fat (0.1 g/100 g), and high sugar (21.2 g/100 g) can adversely affect LDL cholesterol and triglyceride levels.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Refined sugar (21.2 g/100 g), refined wheat flour (low fibre 1.3 g/100 g), and trace trans fat (0.1 g/100 g) are all pro-inflammatory.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid regular consumption of banana cake, as the high refined sugar, low fibre, and trace trans fat content promote systemic inflammatory mediators; if consumed, limit to a very small portion and pair with anti-inflammatory foods.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Banana cake is a moist, sweet baked cake made from ripe mashed bananas combined with wheat flour, butter or vegetable oil, eggs, sugar, baking soda or baking powder, and salt. The ripe bananas provide natural sweetness, moisture, and a characteristic soft, dense crumb texture with a tender golden-brown crust. It is widely enjoyed as a snack or dessert across Singapore, Malaysia, and internationally. Per 100 g, banana cake provides 324 kcal, 5.6 g protein, 17.4 g fat (3.0 g saturated, 0.1 g trans), 36.2 g carbohydrate, 21.2 g sugar, 1.3 g dietary fibre, and 143 mg sodium. The glycaemic index is 51 (low GI) but the glycaemic load per 100 g serving is 18.5 (medium-high), making portion control critical. Clinically, the high sugar content (21.2 g/100 g) exceeds the 20 g/100 g threshold relevant to type 2 diabetes management, and the low fibre content limits glycaemic buffering. The combination of refined sugar, saturated fat, and trace trans fat raises moderate cardiovascular concern. Banana cake is not suitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes or rheumatoid arthritis without strict portion limitation. It contains gluten (wheat flour) and eggs as major allergens, and dairy if butter is used.

Is banana cake high in sugar?
What is the GI of banana cake?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo at confidence 0.95 (exact match). Secondary micronutrients (cholesterol, saturated fat, trans fat, vitamin C) supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy at confidence 0.70. GI value of 51 confirmed in University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables. Overall record confidence is high given primary source alignment.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for banana cake is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for this product as of 2026. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo record for banana cake and were not available in the USDA SR Legacy closest analogue at the level of confidence required. Cholesterol (40 mg), saturated fat (3.0 g), trans fat (0.1 g), and vitamin C (1.0 mg) were sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (banana cake analogue) at confidence 0.70 as these fields were absent from the HPB record. All core macros are non-zero and reflect the genuine nutritional composition of this baked product containing bananas, flour, fat, eggs, and sugar — no zero-macro justification is required.
